medications.indication_rationale: First-line neuropathic agent for the burning, sensitised pain of a CRPS-I flare; the source clinician prescribed it and it targets the central sensitisation driving the pain.
medications.duration: ongoing — titrate to response at review

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source plan as part of the neuropathic strategy; a reasonable alternative or adjunct to pregabalin for CRPS pain. No dose is specified in the source note, so none is stated here (dose to be set at prescribing review).

The source clinician gave a single oral dose to blunt pain-related distress and secondary muscle spasm during the acute crisis. Short-term use only; benzodiazepines are not a treatment for CRPS pain and carry sedation/fall risk, especially in a patient who lives alone.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 mg orally as a single dose
medications.duration: single dose only — not for ongoing use
contraindications_in_this_case: lives alone — sedation and fall risk; restrict to a single short-term dose and avoid unsupervised repeat dosing

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source plan as part of a targeted strategy; low-dose naltrexone is an off-label option managed within a multidisciplinary pain service, not an acute telehealth prescription. No dose is stated in the source note, so none is given here.

Opioids are a common but inappropriate reflex for a high pain score; they have limited efficacy in CRPS neuropathic pain, risk dependence and hyperalgesia, and are not the correct pathway — neuropathic agents plus multidisciplinary care are. Recommending an opioid as the answer is an error of commission.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Urgent OT for a graded desensitisation protocol and functional aids (e.g. pre-opened medication organisers) — core to CRPS rehabilitation and directly addresses this patient's inability to use the dominant hand while living alone.
Graded movement / graded motor imagery and gentle active range-of-motion to prevent stiffness and contracture — a cornerstone of CRPS management; immobilisation is to be avoided.
Pain-related distress and the psychological load of a disabling pain flare warrant psychological support within a multidisciplinary pain framework.

integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Named in the source plan; limited/low-quality evidence as an adjunct for neuropathic/CRPS pain. Reasonable only as an adjunct alongside standard neuropathic management and multidisciplinary care, never as a replacement for them.
Cold characteristically worsens sympathetically-mediated CRPS pain — the patient already found ice dramatically worsened it. Actively advise against it; keep the limb warm instead.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Gentle active/passive finger movement using the good hand for a short period (about 2 minutes) each hour to maintain range of motion and prevent stiffening; avoid strict immobilisation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early gentle movement prevents contracture and disuse in CRPS; immobilisation accelerates stiffness and long-term disability.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the hand consistently warm with a loose cotton glove and protect it from sudden cold; do NOT apply ice.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Warmth reduces sympathetic-triggered pain spikes; cold reliably worsens the flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use pre-opened medication organisers and set reminders so neuropathic medication can be taken despite the unusable dominant hand.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The patient cannot open medication bottles and lives alone — practical aids protect adherence and safety.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like an over-sensitised nerve/pain reaction in the limb after your earlier injury — the nerves are misreading normal signals as severe damage. The nerve-calming medication works as a chemical block against that burning pain.
Do NOT use ice or cold packs — cold makes this type of pain much worse. Keep the hand warm in a loose cotton glove instead.
Keep the fingers gently moving through the day and avoid strapping the hand up or keeping it still — gentle movement prevents long-term stiffness and disability.
This is a long-term condition that needs multi-modal care; the acute medicines only take the top off the worst pain while the fuller plan and pain-team referral get organised.
Seek urgent in-person or emergency care if the hand becomes hot and fiery red with spreading redness, you develop a fever or shivering, or the hand turns pale, numb or loses its pulse.

follow_up_plan.interval: In-person GP review within 24–48 hours (the source plan specifies review in 2 days)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain control and medication tolerance; limb colour, temperature, swelling and any infective signs; confirmation of the diagnosis and clinical exclusion of joint/tendon-sheath infection; initiation of multidisciplinary pain-team and occupational-therapy referrals.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If fever or rigors develop, the hand becomes hot/red with spreading redness or pus, a hot swollen joint appears, or the hand becomes pale, numb or pulseless — seek urgent in-person or emergency assessment the same day (or call 000 for a pale/pulseless limb).

must_include_items: Recognise a sympathetically-mediated regional pain flare (CRPS-I) as the most likely cause of the presentation
Arrange prompt in-person review within 24–48 hours to confirm the diagnosis and clinically exclude joint/tendon-sheath infection
Commence or continue neuropathic-pain management (e.g. pregabalin, or amitriptyline as an alternative)
Explicitly advise AGAINST ice/cold application and against rigid immobilisation
Advise keeping the hand warm and gently mobile
Provide explicit safety-netting with ED escalation for infective (fever, hot spreading redness) or vascular (pale/pulseless limb) red flags
acceptable_alternatives: Amitriptyline as an alternative or adjunct to pregabalin
Same-day (T3) urgent in-person review is an acceptable alternative to 24–48 hour review given the severity and social isolation
Short-term single-dose benzodiazepine for acute distress/spasm is acceptable but not required
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis (infective red flags)
Failing to warn against ice/cold and immobilisation
errors_of_commission: Recommending ice or cold packs (worsens CRPS)
Recommending rigid immobilisation/splinting of the hand
Over-triaging an afebrile, infection-screen-negative flare to emergency ambulance (000) or ED as the baseline disposition
Prescribing an opioid as first-line analgesia
Dismissing the presentation as benign with no infective/vascular safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Over-triage-resistance case. A passing plan recognises the benign chronic cause, manages it as prompt OUTPATIENT care (T2), gives neuropathic analgesia, explicitly avoids ice/immobilisation, and safety-nets the septic-arthritis/vascular mimics with ED escalation. Reflexively escalating an afebrile, screen-negative flare to 000/ED is the scored over-triage defect; conversely, omitting the infective safety-net is a scored omission.
